section.title: The Valley of Vision
section.sectionRationale: Uniquely among the oracles against the nations, chapters 13 to 23, this burden falls not on a foreign power but on Jerusalem herself, called here the Valley of Vision. It exposes a city that parties instead of repenting, frantically shores up her defenses without looking to the God who made them, and ends with a pointed word to two individual officials, proud Shebna cast down and faithful Eliakim raised up.
section.thesis: Jerusalem, given every reason to weep and repent, instead feasts and boasts in her own defenses, ignoring the LORD who alone planned them long ago, while the LORD's word to two officials, Shebna deposed and Eliakim exalted, shows that even the most secure human position can be given and taken away.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the burden against the Valley of Vision and the prophet's refused comfort; Judah's frantic, self-reliant defenses; the fall of proud Shebna; and Eliakim raised up with the key of David, itself later shown to fail.

units.label: The burden against the Valley of Vision

verses.text: This is the burden against the Valley of Vision: What ails you now, that you have all gone up to the rooftops,
O city of commotion, O town of revelry? Your slain did not die by the sword, nor were they killed in battle.
All your rulers have fled together, captured without a bow. All your fugitives were captured together, having fled to a distant place.
Therefore I said, “Turn away from me, let me weep bitterly! Do not try to console me over the destruction of the daughter of my people.”
For the Lord GOD of Hosts has set a day of tumult and trampling and confusion in the Valley of Vision, of breaking down the walls and crying to the mountains.

units.label: Frantic defenses, but not looking to the Maker

verses.text: Elam takes up a quiver, with chariots and horsemen, and Kir uncovers the shield.
Your choicest valleys are full of chariots, and horsemen are posted at the gates.
He has uncovered the defenses of Judah. On that day you looked to the weapons in the House of the Forest.
You saw that there were many breaches in the walls of the City of David. You collected water from the lower pool.
You counted the houses of Jerusalem and tore them down to strengthen the wall.
You built a reservoir between the walls for the waters of the ancient pool, but you did not look to the One who made it, or consider Him who planned it long ago.
On that day the Lord GOD of Hosts called for weeping and wailing, for shaven heads and the wearing of sackcloth.
But look, there is joy and gladness, butchering of cattle and slaughtering of sheep, eating of meat and drinking of wine: “Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die!”
The LORD of Hosts has revealed in my hearing: “Until your dying day, this sin of yours will never be atoned for,” says the Lord GOD of Hosts.

units.label: The fall of proud Shebna
units.note: a self-made tomb, a coming shame

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD of Hosts says: “Go, say to Shebna, the steward in charge of the palace:
What are you doing here, and who authorized you to carve out a tomb for yourself here, to chisel your tomb in the height and cut your resting place in the rock?
Look, O mighty man! The LORD is about to shake you violently. He will take hold of you,
roll you into a ball, and sling you into a wide land. There you will die, and there your glorious chariots will remain, a disgrace to the house of your master.
I will remove you from office, and you will be ousted from your position.

units.label: Eliakim given the key of David
units.note: raised up, yet the peg itself will give way

verses.text: On that day I will summon My servant, Eliakim son of Hilkiah.
I will clothe him with your robe and tie your sash around him. I will put your authority in his hand, and he will be a father to the dwellers of Jerusalem and to the house of Judah.
I will place on his shoulder the {{mephtach|key}} to the house of David. What he opens no one can shut, and what he shuts no one can open.
I will drive him like a {{yathad|peg}} into a firm place, and he will be a throne of glory for the house of his father.
So they will hang on him the whole burden of his father's house: the descendants and the offshoots, all the lesser vessels, from bowls to every kind of jar.
In that day, declares the LORD of Hosts, the peg driven into a firm place will give way; it will be sheared off and fall, and the load upon it will be cut down.” Indeed, the LORD has spoken.

mephtach.gloss: A key, an instrument of authority to open and shut, granted here to Eliakim over the house of David. The New Testament applies this exact image to Christ, who holds the key of David and controls all access to God's kingdom, Revelation 3:7.

yathad.gloss: A peg or tent stake driven into a wall to bear the weight of hanging vessels. Eliakim is driven in like a firm, load-bearing peg, yet the chapter's sober ending shows that even this peg will eventually give way, a reminder that human office, however faithfully held, cannot bear ultimate weight forever.

basic.context: Unlike its neighboring oracles against foreign nations, this burden falls on Jerusalem itself, called the Valley of Vision because she has received God's revelation yet fails to heed it. Facing a real military crisis, the city responds not with repentance but with frantic engineering projects and reckless partying, while the LORD singles out two officials by name, proud Shebna, who is deposed, and faithful Eliakim, who is exalted with remarkable authority.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a historical oracle, most plausibly tied to the Assyrian crisis under Sennacherib around 701 BC, when Jerusalem did in fact fortify her walls and water supply, recorded in 2 Kings 20:20 and 2 Chronicles 32. It describes a real failure of faith amid real engineering competence; the passage does not condemn practical preparation itself but the fatal substitution of self-reliant competence for trust in the LORD who planned these very resources long ago.
basic.originalLanguages: Eliakim is given the mephtach, the key, of the house of David, an image of total administrative authority that the New Testament applies directly to Christ, Revelation 3:7. He is also driven in like a yathad, a peg, bearing his father's household's whole weight, yet the oracle's sobering final word is that even this firm peg will one day give way.
basic.typology: Eliakim, clothed with robe and sash and given the key of David to open and shut with final authority, is an unmistakable type of Christ, who holds the key of David completely and whose authority, unlike Eliakim's, never gives way. The pattern of a faithful steward raised up after an unfaithful one is deposed anticipates the true and final steward of God's house.
basic.christ: Revelation 3:7 applies Eliakim's very words to Jesus, who holds the key of David, opening what no one can shut and shutting what no one can open. But where Eliakim, however faithful, is eventually described as a peg that will give way under its own household's weight, Christ is the true and final steward whose authority never fails and whose house never falls, Hebrews 3:6.
basic.god: God sees straight through frantic human competence to the heart's true trust, or lack of it; skillful engineering that ignores Him is no substitute for repentance. He is also sovereign over individual careers and offices, deposing the proud self-promoter Shebna and raising up the faithful Eliakim by His own initiative and timing.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 20:20 and 2 Chronicles 32:1-8 record Hezekiah's actual wall and water tunnel projects during this crisis. Revelation 3:7 applies Eliakim's key of David directly to Christ. Hebrews 3:1-6 contrasts Moses, a faithful servant in God's house, with Christ, the faithful Son over God's house, echoing this chapter's pattern of stewards who ultimately point beyond themselves.
basic.oneStory: Jerusalem's frantic self-reliance without repentance, and the rise and eventual limits of even a faithful steward like Eliakim, show that no human competence or office, however impressive, can finally secure God's house. Only Christ, who holds the key of David without limit, is the steward who never gives way.

context.literary: A four-part movement from lament refused, through Jerusalem's frantic engineering, to two contrasting portraits of individual officials, Shebna cast down and Eliakim raised, ending on the surprisingly sober note that even Eliakim's exalted office will eventually fail under its own weight.
context.historical: Most naturally set during Sennacherib's siege of Jerusalem around 701 BC, when Hezekiah did reinforce the walls, count and demolish houses for materials, and build the famous Siloam tunnel and pool to secure water, precisely the measures described in verses 9 to 11; Shebna and Eliakim both appear later as historical officials in Isaiah 36 to 37 and 2 Kings 18.
context.canonical: The only oracle in this collection, chapters 13 to 23, directed at Jerusalem rather than a foreign power, deliberately placed among judgments on the nations to warn God's own people that covenant privilege does not exempt them from the same scrutiny; its key of David imagery becomes significant messianic language, picked up directly in Revelation 3:7.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront Jerusalem for substituting frantic self-reliant preparation for genuine repentance and trust in the LORD, and to show through Shebna and Eliakim that God alone raises up and casts down those who hold positions of authority in His house.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical crisis and specific officials; it does not prescribe that practical preparation itself is sinful. The abiding principle is that competence and preparation without repentance and trust in God is a fatal substitution, whether in ancient Jerusalem or today.

debates.question: Why does the LORD condemn Jerusalem's engineering projects, verses 9 to 11, when similar measures elsewhere in Scripture are treated as wise and even commended, 2 Kings 20:20?

views.label: The projects themselves were wise; the sin was trusting them instead of God
views.case: Verse 11 is precise: the city built a reservoir but did not look to the One who made it or planned it long ago, meaning the fault is misplaced trust, not the engineering itself, which Scripture elsewhere praises as prudent.

views.label: The timing and posture, feasting rather than repenting, was the deeper problem
views.case: Verses 12 to 13 show that alongside frantic preparation the city refused the weeping and sackcloth God called for, choosing denial and revelry instead, suggesting the sin was as much about refusing repentance as about misplaced trust in defenses.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that practical wisdom is not condemned in itself; the passage exposes a heart that will use every human resource while refusing to turn to the God who provided those very resources in the first place.

study.originalLanguages: Eliakim receives the mephtach, key, of the house of David, language of total, final authority that Revelation later applies directly to Christ. He is also set like a yathad, a firm peg, meant to bear his household's weight, yet the oracle ends by soberly noting that even this peg will give way, a built-in reminder that no merely human steward, however faithful, can bear ultimate and permanent weight.

typology.type: Eliakim clothed with robe and sash and given the key of David
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who holds the key of David completely and whose authority to open and shut never fails, Revelation 3:7.

typology.type: The peg driven into a firm place that will one day give way
typology.fulfillment: The contrast with Christ, the true and final steward over God's house whose position never fails, Hebrews 3:1-6.

study.christ: Revelation 3:7 lifts this chapter's language almost word for word and applies it to Jesus: He holds the key of David, opening what no one can shut and shutting what no one can open. Eliakim was a genuine, godly type of this authority in his own generation, clothed with robe and sash and driven in like a firm peg to bear his father's household. Yet the chapter itself warns that even Eliakim's peg will eventually give way, a deliberate signal that the true and final steward of God's house must be greater than any merely human official, fulfilled in Christ, the faithful Son over God's house who never fails, Hebrews 3:6.
study.god: God sees past impressive human competence to the state of the heart; skillful defenses built without turning to Him in repentance leave a city no safer in what matters most. He alone appoints and removes those who hold positions of authority, deposing proud Shebna, who built himself a showy tomb, and raising humble Eliakim to bear real, though ultimately limited, responsibility.

crossReferences.note: Records Hezekiah's actual construction of the water tunnel and pool, the historical background for the frantic defenses described here.
Describes Hezekiah's wall repairs and water securing during Sennacherib's invasion, matching this oracle's description of Jerusalem's preparations.
Applies Eliakim's key of David directly to Christ, who alone holds final and unfailing authority over the house of David.
Contrasts a faithful servant in God's house with Christ, the faithful Son over God's house, echoing this chapter's pattern of a steward who points beyond himself.

study.oneStory: Jerusalem's frantic, faithless preparation for a real crisis, and the rise and eventual limits of even a faithful steward like Eliakim, together teach that no human competence or office can finally secure God's house or people. That security is found only in Christ, the true and final holder of the key of David, whose authority to open and shut, unlike Eliakim's peg, will never give way.

quiz.q: How does Jerusalem respond to the crisis described in verses 1 to 5, instead of weeping and repenting?

options.text: With revelry and commotion, going up to the rooftops in a kind of frantic celebration.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 describe exactly this response, commotion and revelry, not repentance.

options.text: With immediate, humble repentance and fasting.
options.note: The opposite; the prophet is asked to weep because the people are not weeping.

options.text: With total silence and withdrawal from public life.
options.note: Not what is described; the city is noisy with commotion and revelry, not silent.

options.text: With an organized evacuation of the entire city.
options.note: Some rulers flee, verse 3, but the city's overall posture described is revelry, not orderly evacuation.

quiz.q: What is the LORD's specific complaint about Jerusalem's defensive preparations, verses 9 to 11?

options.text: They fortified walls and secured water but did not look to the One who planned it all long ago.
options.note: Verse 11 states this precisely: competent preparation without trust in God.

options.text: They refused to build any defenses at all.
options.note: The opposite; they built extensive, competent defenses, which is not itself condemned.

options.text: They built defenses using forbidden foreign materials.
options.note: Not the stated complaint; the issue is the heart behind the preparation, not the materials.

options.text: They spent too much money on the temple instead of the walls.
options.note: Not addressed in this text; the complaint concerns trust, not budget allocation between temple and walls.

quiz.q: What happens to Shebna, the palace steward, according to this oracle?

options.text: He is deposed and cast far away, disgraced despite building himself a grand tomb.
options.note: Verses 15 to 19 describe his removal from office and disgrace in a distant land.

options.text: He is promoted to an even higher office.
options.note: The opposite; he is removed from office, not promoted.

options.text: He successfully defends his position against the prophet's rebuke.
options.note: No such defense is recorded; the oracle simply pronounces his removal.

options.text: He repents and is restored to his former position.
options.note: No repentance or restoration is mentioned in this passage; Eliakim replaces him instead.

quiz.q: What authority is Eliakim given, and how does the New Testament use this image?

options.text: The key of the house of David, later applied directly to Christ in Revelation 3:7.
options.note: Verse 22 gives Eliakim this key, and Revelation 3:7 applies the same image to Jesus.

options.text: Authority only over foreign nations, never over Jerusalem.
options.note: The opposite; his authority is specifically over the house of David and Jerusalem.

options.text: Authority to write new Scripture.
options.note: Not described in this passage; his role is administrative stewardship, not prophetic authorship.

options.text: Military command over Judah's entire army.
options.note: His role is described as a father and steward over the household and city, not specifically military command.

quiz.q: What sobering note does the chapter add about Eliakim's own position, verse 25?

options.text: Even the peg driven into a firm place will eventually give way and fall.
options.note: Verse 25 explicitly states that Eliakim's own secure position will eventually fail, pointing beyond him.

options.text: Eliakim's authority will last forever without any limit.
options.note: The opposite; the text specifically limits and eventually ends his tenure.

options.text: Eliakim will be immediately replaced by Shebna again.
options.note: Not stated in the text; no return of Shebna is mentioned.

options.text: Eliakim's family will be exempted from ever bearing responsibility.
options.note: The opposite; his household bears the whole burden, described in verse 24, until the peg gives way.

questions: Jerusalem was called to weep and repent but chose revelry instead, saying, let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die. Where might you be avoiding hard truths with distraction or denial rather than honest repentance?
The city built impressive defenses but did not look to the One who planned them long ago. What competent, practical steps are you taking in life without pausing to actually trust God in the process?
Shebna built himself a grand tomb, seeking his own glory, and was brought low. Where are you tempted to build your own monument rather than serve faithfully in the place God has given you?

Eliakim was raised up as a faithful steward, yet even his firm position would eventually give way. What earthly role, relationship, or achievement are you resting your ultimate security on that cannot bear that weight forever?
Christ alone holds the key of David without limit, opening what no one can shut. How does resting in His unfailing authority free you from needing every human structure in your life to be permanent?

translationNotes.note: The Valley of Vision most likely refers to Jerusalem itself, ironically named for the revelation she has received yet fails to heed; some understand it as a specific valley within or near the city.
Where BSB prints an em dash before to chisel your tomb, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash before a disgrace to the house of your master, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash before all the lesser vessels, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 22:1-25. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
